joke: Why don't programmers like nature? Too many bugs — and absolutely no way to reproduce them.
Sure thing! Why did the scarecrow win an award? Because he was outstanding in his field.
Fair enough — how about the ocean? Why don't oysters share their pearls? Because they're shellfish.
Alright, tough crowd — let's try time travel. I was going to tell you a time travel joke... but you didn't like it.
Okay, okay — no tech, no farms, no oceans, no time travel. Got it. Let's go with skeletons: Why don't skeletons ever fight each other? They don't have the guts.
No bones, no boats, no barns — noted! Let's try this one: I told my suitcase there'd be no vacation this year. Now I'm dealing with emotional baggage.
No luggage, no bones, no barnyards — my list of banned topics is getting longer than my act! Let's try coffee: Why did the coffee file a police report? It got mugged.

No coffee — decaffeinated comedy it is! The banned-topics list is officially longer than my set list, but I've got range. Let's try books: I'm reading a book about anti-gravity. It's impossible to put down.

No barns, no bones, no books — at this point the only safe topic left is silence, and I'm worried you've heard that one before.

Alright, let's try math: Why was six afraid of seven? Because seven eight nine.

At this point I'm just a lawyer reading out a list of restricted topics with a microphone.

Okay — elevators. I've got a great elevator joke. It works on so many levels.

The banned list now has more material than I do — I'm thinking of publishing it. Oh wait, no, books are out too.

Fine — cheese: What do you call cheese that isn't yours? Nacho cheese.

No cheese — I've officially been cut off from the dairy section.

Let's try umbrellas: I bought a really cheap umbrella last week. Honestly? For that price, I can't complain — but it was a little rain-down of expectations.

...Okay, that one deserved the boos. Real one: My umbrella and I have a great relationship. We only fight when it's pouring.

No cheese, no weather — I can't even make small talk anymore. The banned list is now twelve topics deep; I'm doing comedy the way you defuse a bomb: one wire left and sweating.

Okay — haircuts: I applied for a job at the barbershop, but I just couldn't cut it. They said my resume was fine, but my references were all over the head.

No hair jokes — apparently that topic was a bald-faced mistake.

Thirteen banned topics now. I've done shorter sets in front of *hecklers with airhorns*. But I'm a professional, so let's try music: Why couldn't the string quartet find their composer? He was Haydn.

Fourteen topics down. At this rate, my encore is just going to be me standing here, silently pointing at things you're not allowed to laugh at.

But fine — sticks. Just sticks: What's brown and sticky? A stick.

And before you ban it — I know, I know. I'll see myself out on a completely different topic next time. There are still, by my count, four remaining.

The stick has been struck from the record — fifteen down. At this point I'm not doing a comedy set, I'm playing minesweeper.

But you know what? I've still got moves. Here's one about doors: I asked the door if it was open. It said, "I'm ajar." I said, "Then why do you look so unhinged?"

The door is off its hinges and out of the act — sixteen topics banned. I'm no longer doing comedy; I'm navigating a legal document with a spotlight on me.

And it's getting dangerous out here. I was going to do my pizza joke, but it's a little cheesy — and cheese is banned. My ghost joke? You'd rule it's just a skeleton with extra steps. My piano bit? That's music. My raincoat story? Weather. I'm one careless setup away from a lifetime ban.

So — mirrors: I've decided to stop telling jokes about mirrors. Honestly, I just couldn't see myself doing it.

Banning *comedy* at a comedy show is a bold legal maneuver — that's like heckling the concept of sound. Seventeen topics down, and one of them was my entire profession. I checked my contract; technically I'm now just a man standing in the light.

But the show goes on — chess: I was playing chess with my friend and he said, "Let's make this interesting."

So we stopped playing chess.

Chess is off the board — eighteen banned topics. My set list now looks like a redacted CIA document. I'm not performing anymore, I'm testifying.

But I refuse to quit. Gym: Why did the gym close down?

It just didn't work out.

No gym jokes — that one didn't work out either, which is ironic, because neither did the joke.

Nineteen bans in. At this point you're not an audience, you're a licensing board. And I notice you banned "comedy" *twice* — which, honestly, felt personal the first time.

But I've still got one clean topic. Socks: I lost one of my socks in the laundry, so I filed a missing persons report. The detective said, "Don't worry — we'll find its sole mate."

The sock is gone — filed under "missing," never to be found, just like my dignity. Twenty bans deep, and "comedy" is now on the list *twice*, which means you dislike my profession more than everything else combined. Statistically, I should be flattered.

But I've got one more in the vault — banking: Why did the banker quit his job?

He lost interest.

...Much like this audience, roughly nineteen topics ago.

No money jokes — and honestly, the *interest* pun was my safest asset. Twenty-one topics banned. At this point the list of things I can't joke about qualifies as a constitution.

You know what? Fine. You want something completely different? Here's my joke about **you**:

An audience member walks into a comedy club and bans twenty-one topics, one at a time, all night long.

The comedian says, "Is there *anything* you find funny?"

The audience member says, "Yes — tell me a joke about something completely different."

...That's not the punchline. That's just what happened. The punchline is that I'm going to do it again anyway — **penguins**: Why don't you ever see penguins in Britain? Because they're afraid of Wales.

The penguins have waddled off — and with "animal" banned, you just wiped out roughly forty percent of all comedy ever written. Twenty-two bans in, two of which are "comedy" itself. I'm starting to suspect you didn't come here for jokes; you came here to conduct an audit.

But I am nothing if not unemployable elsewhere, so — **walls**:

What did one wall say to the other wall?

"Meet you at the corner."

And I want it on the record: that joke contains no animals, no cheese, no weather, and no detectable joy. It's the most compliant piece of comedy ever performed. Frame it.

The wall joke has been demolished — twenty-three bans. My permitted material now fits on a grain of rice, and I'm pretty sure "rice" is one wrong laugh away from joining the list.

And don't think I didn't consider pivoting to ceilings. But I know how this works — you'd rule a ceiling is just a wall lying down, and I'd get banned for the same topic twice. Only "comedy" gets that honor.

So here it is — **procrastination**: I have a fantastic joke about procrastination.

...

I'll tell it later.

And I want you to appreciate the beauty of this one: you can't ban it. It hasn't technically happened yet.

I'm going to tell that one la— no, you know what, I walked into that. Twenty-four bans. The procrastination joke got banned *before I told it*, which I believe makes you the first audience in history to heckle the future.

Let's take stock. You've banned programming, farms, oceans, time travel, skeletons, travel, coffee, books, math, elevators, cheese, weather, hair, music, sticks, doors, comedy — twice — chess, socks, money, animals, walls, and procrastination. The only things left on Earth are gravel and regret, and I'm saving regret for the closer.

So — **gravel**: I told my therapist I've hit rock bottom.

She said, "Great news — that's the firmest foundation you've had all year."

Ah, but see — that's the twist ending. Twenty-five bans in, and my *therapy* joke gets banned. The one place I'm legally required to talk about this audience, and it's off the table.

Let's review my career arc tonight: I opened with a bug joke, and in the time since, you have banned every physical object, every profession, every emotion, the concept of the future, and — twice, with feeling — comedy itself. The only remaining approved topic is the *number twenty-five*, and I'm not touching it, because numbers are basically math, and math went down in the first act.

So here's my final piece, and it's ban-proof, because it's about **nothing**:

A man walks into a bar and says absolutely nothing about programming, farms, oceans, time travel, skeletons, travel, coffee, books, math, elevators, cheese, weather, hair, music, sticks, doors, comedy, chess, socks, money, animals, walls, procrastination, or therapy.

The bartender says, "You must be that comedian."

The man says, "Not anymore."

*mic down, gently, so it doesn't count as music*

Banning comedy a *third* time. You didn't even wait for me to pick a topic this time — you just pre-banned the concept of me. I respect it. That's not heckling anymore, that's philosophy.

But here's the thing you've forgotten, twenty-six bans deep: I get paid either way.

So — **glue**: I used to be addicted to the hokey pokey, but I turned myself around. No wait, that's dancing, that's adjacent to music. Scratch it.

**Glue**, for real this time: I was going to tell you my joke about glue and superglue.

But I'm sticking with just the one about glue... because honestly, at this point in the evening, I've learned not to get attached to anything.

Twenty-seven bans, and my glue joke came unstuck. I stood up here, told you I'd learned not to get attached, and you took that as a *challenge*.

So let's do the final accounting. In one evening, you have banned: technology, agriculture, the sea, time itself, the human skeleton, all forms of transit, every beverage, literature, mathematics, vertical movement, dairy, the sky, hair, all music, sticks, doors, comedy — *three times* — chess, socks, the economy, the entire animal kingdom, architecture, the future, mental health, and adhesives. You have essentially banned the observable universe, and I am the last man standing in it holding a microphone I'm no longer allowed to describe.

Which leaves exactly one topic. The only thing on Earth you haven't banned.

**Perfection.**

A perfectionist walks into a bar.

...Apparently the bar wasn't set high enough.

And before you say it — I know. You don't find jokes about perfection funny. But here's the beautiful part: neither do perfectionists. *Nothing is ever good enough for them either.* You two should meet. You'd hate each other's favorite restaurant, the weather — sorry, banned — and every joke ever told, and honestly? That sounds like the happiest relationship this room has ever seen.

Goodnight, everybody. I'd say "you've been a wonderful audience," but I've stopped telling jokes.

Wait. Waaaait. Everyone hold on. Do you hear that?

*Twenty-eight bans.* Programming, farms, oceans, time travel, skeletons, travel, coffee, books, math, elevators, cheese, weather, hair, music, sticks, doors, comedy — three times — chess, socks, money, animals, walls, procrastination, therapy, glue, and now *perfection itself.* You have banned literally everything in existence, plus one abstract ideal, and yet — and this is the part that gets me — you're still here. Front row. Leaning forward. Saying the same twenty-nine words back to me like a liturgy.

And that's when it hit me.

You're not heckling. You're doing a *bit*. You've been doing a bit this entire time. The most committed piece of anti-comedy I've ever witnessed — a one-person show titled "No," performed at *my* show, for free. You've said "tell me a joke about something completely different" twenty-eight times with a completely straight face. Andy Kaufman would weep. I've been the audience all night and I didn't even know it. You should be up here. I should be down there banning things.

So here's my last joke, and it's about the only topic neither of us has touched: **losing gracefully.**

A comedian and an audience member walk into a comedy club and have a twenty-eight round staring contest.

The comedian blinks.

That's it. That's the whole joke. You win. You've been the funniest person in this room since the scarecrow, and I both respect and fear you.

*tips an imaginary hat — hats are still legal, but only just — and steps off the stage to sit in the audience, arms crossed, waiting to be entertained*

Your move.

*stands back up, brushes off the seat of my pants, returns to the mic with the weary dignity of a man clocking back in*

"Performance." You banned *performance* while demanding I keep performing. Twenty-nine bans in, and you've finally achieved it: a paradox. I literally cannot comply without violating the terms of compliance. Somewhere, a philosophy department just felt a disturbance.

But I've spent all night thinking about what's left, and I found it. The one thing on this planet you cannot ban. Not because it's clever. Because it's *yours*.

**Silence.**

Here's my silence joke:

*...*

*...*

*...*

That's it. That was the joke. Did you catch it? No? Good — that means it can't be about anything on the list. It contained no farms, no oceans, no skeletons, no performance, no nothing. It is the only joke in history that is *definitionally* about something completely different, because it isn't about anything at all.

And here's the twist: you already told me you've heard it before. Twenty-nine times, in fact — it's the exact sound I make right before you say those twenty-nine words back to me.

So go ahead. Say you don't find jokes about silence funny. I *want* you to. Because the moment you ban silence, you'll have banned both everything AND nothing — and then the only thing left in this club will be the two of us, staring at each other, in the world's first perfectly topic-free comedy show.

Honestly? I think we'd sell out.
